days.title: Food on the table: naming what we eat

grammar.title: What is this? Naming food
grammar.explanation:
Russian has **no word for "a" or "the"** — a noun stands alone. To name something, use **Это** ("this is"). The noun stays in its dictionary form (the **nominative case**) here, because it is the *subject* being identified, not an object of an action.

Notice the **gender** of these nouns, which matters for later lessons:
- nouns ending in a consonant are **masculine**: **хлеб**, **сыр**, **суп**
- nouns ending in **-о** are **neuter**: **мясо**, **яблоко**
- nouns ending in **-а** are **feminine**: **еда**

examples.english: This is soup. It is tasty.
This is meat and an apple.

reading.title: На столе (On the table)
reading.text: — Это хлеб? — Да, это хлеб и сыр. Это вкусно!
reading.translation: "Is this bread?" "Yes, this is bread and cheese. It is tasty!"

days.speakingPrompt: Point at three foods around you (or imagine them) and say what each one is using "Это ...". For example: "Это хлеб. Это сыр. Это вкусно!"

days.title: The verb «есть»: I eat something

grammar.title: «есть» + the accusative object
grammar.explanation:
When a noun is the **direct object** — the thing being eaten — it goes into the **accusative case**.

Good news for now: for **masculine inanimate** nouns (like хлеб, сыр, суп) and **neuter** nouns (like мясо, яблоко), the accusative form looks **exactly the same as the dictionary form**. Nothing changes!

The verb **есть** (to eat) changes its ending by person:
- я **ем** (I eat)
- ты **ешь** (you eat, informal)

examples.english: You are eating bread and cheese.

reading.title: Завтрак (Breakfast)
reading.text: — Ты ешь хлеб? — Да. Я ем хлеб и сыр. — Я тоже ем сыр.
reading.translation: "Are you eating bread?" "Yes. I am eating bread and cheese." "I am eating cheese too."

days.speakingPrompt: Say what you are eating for breakfast right now using «Я ем ...» and one of today's foods. Then ask a friend «Ты ешь ...?» about a different food.

days.title: Feminine objects: «еда» becomes «еду»

grammar.title: Accusative of feminine nouns: -а → -у
grammar.explanation:
Feminine nouns ending in **-а** *do* change in the accusative: the **-а becomes -у**.

- **еда** (food) → Я ем **еду**
- **рыба** (fish) → Я ем **рыбу**
- **каша** (porridge) → Я ем **кашу**
- **колбаса** (sausage) → Я ем **колбасу**

The verb **есть** continues: он/она **ест** (he/she eats).

So: **masculine inanimate** and **neuter** = no change; **feminine -а** = **-у**.

examples.english: I am eating sausage and bread.

reading.title: Кто что ест (Who eats what)
reading.text: Он ест кашу. Она ест рыбу. Я ем хлеб и колбасу.
reading.translation: He is eating porridge. She is eating fish. I am eating bread and sausage.

days.speakingPrompt: Describe three people (он, она, я) eating three different foods. Use at least one feminine noun in the accusative, for example «Она ест рыбу».

days.title: The verb «пить»: I drink something

grammar.title: «пить» + accusative drinks
grammar.explanation:
The verb **пить** (to drink) also takes a **direct object in the accusative**:
- я **пью** (I drink)
- ты **пьёшь** (you drink, informal)
- он/она **пьёт** (he/she drinks)

The same accusative rules apply to drinks:
- **чай**, **кофе**, **сок** — masculine, no change (note: **кофе** never changes its ending at all)
- **вода** (water) — feminine, so **-а → -у**: Я пью **воду**.

examples.english: Are you drinking coffee or juice?

reading.title: Чай или кофе? (Tea or coffee?)
reading.text: — Ты пьёшь чай? — Нет, я пью кофе. — А я пью воду.
reading.translation: "Are you drinking tea?" "No, I am drinking coffee." "And I am drinking water."

days.speakingPrompt: Say what you usually drink in the morning using «Я пью ...». Include water once (remember: «воду»). Then ask «Ты пьёшь чай или кофе?»

days.title: The verb «читать»: reading menus and more

grammar.title: «читать» + accusative — a regular verb
grammar.explanation:
**читать** (to read) is a typical **-ать verb** with very regular endings:
- я чита**ю**
- ты чита**ешь**
- он/она чита**ет**

Its object is in the **accusative**, following the same rules:
- **меню** — borrowed neuter word, **never changes**
- **книга → книгу**, **газета → газету** (feminine **-а → -у**)

reading.title: В кафе (At the café)
reading.text: Я читаю меню и пью чай. Она читает книгу. Он ест суп.
reading.translation: I am reading the menu and drinking tea. She is reading a book. He is eating soup.

days.speakingPrompt: Imagine you are in a café. Say one sentence with «читать», one with «пить», and one with «есть», each with a different object. For example: «Я читаю меню, пью кофе и ем хлеб.»

days.title: Putting it together: «я люблю» + accusative

grammar.title: Liking food, and asking «Что?»
grammar.explanation:
**любить** (to like/love) also takes an object in the **accusative**:
- я **люблю**, ты **любишь**

You can add **очень** (very much) before it: Я **очень** люблю сыр.

To ask **"What?"** use **Что** — note it is a question word and does not change here:
- **Что ты ешь?** — What are you eating?
- **Что ты пьёшь?** — What are you drinking?

Feminine **-а → -у** still applies: пицц**а** → Она ест пицц**у**. Neuter **молоко** does not change.

examples.english: What are you eating for lunch?

examples.russian: Она ест пиццу и пьёт молоко.
examples.english: She is eating pizza and drinking milk.

reading.title: Обед (Lunch)
reading.text: — Что ты ешь? — Я ем пиццу. — Я очень люблю пиццу! Я тоже ем пиццу и пью молоко.
reading.translation: "What are you eating?" "I am eating pizza." "I love pizza very much! I am eating pizza too and drinking milk."

days.speakingPrompt: Answer the question «Что ты любишь?» Name two foods and one drink you like, using «Я люблю ...» and «Я очень люблю ...». Remember feminine endings (пиццу, воду).

days.title: Review: Food, drink, and the accusative

grammar.title: Week 9 recap: the accusative direct object
grammar.explanation:
This week you learned the **accusative case** for the **direct object** (the thing being eaten, drunk, or read), with three verbs: **есть** (eat), **пить** (drink), **читать** (read), plus **любить** (like).

**Accusative endings for inanimate nouns:**
- **masculine** (хлеб, сыр, суп, чай, сок) → **no change**
- **neuter** (мясо, яблоко, молоко, меню) → **no change**
- **feminine -а** (вода, рыба, пицца, книга) → **-а changes to -у**

**Verb forms learned:** я ем / ты ешь / он ест; я пью / ты пьёшь / он пьёт; я читаю / ты читаешь / он читает; я люблю / ты любишь.

examples.russian: Я ем суп и пью воду.
examples.english: I am eating soup and drinking water.

examples.russian: Она читает книгу и пьёт чай.
examples.english: She is reading a book and drinking tea.

examples.russian: Что ты любишь? — Я люблю пиццу.
examples.english: What do you like? — I like pizza.

reading.title: В кафе (At the café)
reading.text: — Что ты ешь? — Я ем суп и хлеб. А ты пьёшь чай? — Нет, я пью воду. Я читаю меню. — Я очень люблю кофе!
reading.translation: "What are you eating?" "I am eating soup and bread. And are you drinking tea?" "No, I am drinking water. I am reading the menu." "I love coffee very much!"

days.speakingPrompt: Role-play a short café conversation with yourself. Ask and answer «Что ты ешь?» and «Что ты пьёшь?», then say one thing you love using «Я очень люблю ...». Use at least one feminine noun in the accusative (воду, пиццу, рыбу).
